coredata.dc:title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of melamine in milk by a zirconium-based metal-organic frameworks composite
coredata.dc:creator: Lin, Chunhua
Zhong, Caijun
Song, Yonghai
Wang, Li
coredata.dc:description: Melamine has biological toxicity and is forbidden to be used in food processing and food additives. The long-term ingestion of melamine will cause renal malfunction and metabolic problems of the urinary system, so it is of great significance for the rapid and selective detection of melamine. Herein, a zirconium-based metal–organic frameworks (Zr-based MOFs) encapsulated tris(2,2-bipyridyl)ruthenium (II) chloride hexahydrate ([Ru(bpy)3]2+) composite, donated as UiO-66-NH2@Ru, was reported and further studied its sensing performance for melamine based on ratiometric fluorescent method. The UiO-66-NH2@Ru was prepared from ZrCl4, 2-amino-terephthalic acid (TPA-NH2) and [Ru(bpy)3]2+ by one pot reaction through solvothermal method, which exhibited dual emission signals at 445 and 595 nm from single excitation of 350 nm. The corresponding two peaks showed different responses to melamine, that was, the obvious enhancement at 445 nm and the slow decrease at 595 nm, thus achieving the “on–off” ratiometric fluorescence detection. When the concentration of melamine was in the range of 0.27 to 110 μM, the ratio of fluorescence intensity F445/F595 had a good linear relation with the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.995 and the detection limit of 90 nM. The synthetic UiO-66-NH2@Ru probe implemented rapid determination of melamine in real sample of milk powder with high sensitivity and selectivity. Compared with the reported carbon dots, quantum dots, metal nanoclusters, nanocomposites and other fluorescent sensors, MOFs-based sensor have broadened the application of nanomaterials in melamine detection for the first time. This study also demonstrates the Zr-based MOFs as a platform to encapsulate fluorescent dye for constructing ratiometric sensor in the determination of melamine is feasible and exciting.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence probe
Zirconium-based metal-organic frameworks
UiO-66-NH2@Ru
Melamine
Milk

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The feasibility of determining melamine based on UiO-66-NH2@Ru
Fluorescent determination of melamine based on UiO-66-NH2@Ru

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21465015 and 21964010 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The SEM (A) and TEM (B) image of UiO-66-NH2@Ru.
(A) XRD patterns of the simulated UiO-66-NH2, as-prepared UiO-66-NH2 and UiO-66-NH2@Ru. (B) EDS pattern of UiO-66-NH2@Ru. (C) FT-IR spectra of UiO-66-NH2@Ru, [Ru(bpy)3]2+ and TPA-NH2.

(A) UV–visible absorption spectra of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ (red), TPA-NH2 (blue) and UiO-66-NH2@Ru (green). (B) Fluorescence emission diagram of UiO-66-NH2@Ru at different excitation wavelengths. (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ (black), TPA-NH2 (red) and UiO-66-NH2@Ru (green) at λex = 350 nm. (D) Fluorescence stability of UiO-66-NH2@Ru within three weeks.
(A) The response of fluorescence intensity of UiO-66-NH2@Ru to different concentration of melamine at 445 nm and 595 nm. (B) Effect of response time of UiO-66-NH2@Ru to melanine on F445/F595.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of different concentrations of melamine added to UiO-66-NH2@Ru solution. (B) The fluorescence intensity ratio of F445/F595 of 12 μg mL−1 UiO-66-NH2@Ru vs. melamine concentration.
Schematic illustration of the preparation process and the ratiometric fluorescence detection of melamine based on UiO-66-NH2@Ru Probe.
Comparison of the reported fluorescence detection methods for melamine based on nanomaterials.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescence detection of melamine in milk by a zirconium-based metal-organic frameworks composite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Road, Nanchang 330022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Road, Nanchang 330022, China

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Monosaccharide Chemical Synthesis Engineering Research Center, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Road, Nanchang 330022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Monosaccharide Chemical Synthesis Engineering Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: National Monosaccharide Chemical Synthesis Engineering Research Center, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Road, Nanchang 330022, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Jiangxi Normal University, 99 Ziyang Road, Nanchang 330022, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Melamine has biological toxicity and is forbidden to be used in food processing and food additives. The long-term ingestion of melamine will cause renal malfunction and metabolic problems of the urinary system, so it is of great significance for the rapid and selective detection of melamine. Herein, a zirconium-based metal–organic frameworks (Zr-based MOFs) encapsulated tris(2,2-bipyridyl)ruthenium (II) chloride hexahydrate ([Ru(bpy)3]2+) composite, donated as UiO-66-NH2@Ru, was reported and further studied its sensing performance for melamine based on ratiometric fluorescent method. The UiO-66-NH2@Ru was prepared from ZrCl4, 2-amino-terephthalic acid (TPA-NH2) and [Ru(bpy)3]2+ by one pot reaction through solvothermal method, which exhibited dual emission signals at 445 and 595 nm from single excitation of 350 nm. The corresponding two peaks showed different responses to melamine, that was, the obvious enhancement at 445 nm and the slow decrease at 595 nm, thus achieving the “on–off” ratiometric fluorescence detection. When the concentration of melamine was in the range of 0.27 to 110 μM, the ratio of fluorescence intensity F445/F595 had a good linear relation with the correlation coefficient R2 = 0.995 and the detection limit of 90 nM. The synthetic UiO-66-NH2@Ru probe implemented rapid determination of melamine in real sample of milk powder with high sensitivity and selectivity. Compared with the reported carbon dots, quantum dots, metal nanoclusters, nanocomposites and other fluorescent sensors, MOFs-based sensor have broadened the application of nanomaterials in melamine detection for the first time. This study also demonstrates the Zr-based MOFs as a platform to encapsulate fluorescent dye for constructing ratiometric sensor in the determination of melamine is feasible and exciting.

ce:section.ce:para: Melamine is an important nitrogen heterocyclic organic chemical material with a variety of industrial uses. As its nitrogen content is as high as 66.6%, it is often illegally mixed into various dairy products and animal feed as a food additive to artificially increase the total nitrogen content [1,2]. However, melamine is difficult to metabolize, long term and excessive intake of it by humans or animals can induce kidney stones, bladder stones or even death [3-5]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop a simple, rapid and sensitive detection technology for melamine in the field of food safety and bioanalysis, especially in infant formula.

At present, the detection methods of melamine mainly include high performance liquid chromatography/liquid mass spectrometry [6-9], gas chromatography-mass spectrometry [10-13], electrochemical method [14,15], surface enhanced raman spectroscopy [16,17], Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy [18,19], enzyme-linked immunosorbent [20], colorimetric detection [21,22] and so on. However, apart from the special sensitivity and accuracy, most of these methods are relatively high technical requirements, tedious sample pretreatments, long test cycle and high operating cost [1]. Compared with the above methods, fluorescence technology is more attractive because of its high sensitivity, fast response, simple operation and obvious optical phenomenon [23,24]. So far, some fluorescence-based chemical sensors (carbon dots, quantum dots, metal nanoclusters, nanocomposites) for melamine detection have been reported. For instance, Lei et al. used carbon dots as a “turn-on” fluorescent sensor for the determination of melamine based on the anti-quenching ability of Hg2+ to C-dots [25]. Cao and co-workers utilized l-Cys-CdS QDs to sensitively detect melamine through inner filter effect in the presence of citrate-stabilized Au NPs [26]. Kalaiyarasan et al. demonstrated that GSH-Au NCs was used to quantify melamine by ratiometric method through the hydrogen-bonding interaction between melamine and AuNCs [27]. Dai and co-workers reported a nanocomposite composed of C-dots and Au NPs for the selective discrimination of melamine by fluorescence resonance energy transfer [28]. But they were suffered from the disadvantages of complex procedures and/or long response times. As far as we know, melamine sensor based on metal organic frameworks (MOFs) has never been explored.

MOFs, a new porous material, is constructed by coordination of metal ions or metalclusters with multi-functional organic ligands, and possesses controllable framework structure, adjustable pore size and large specific surface area [29,30]. Due to its unique properties, MOFs materials are considered to have promising application prospects as fluorescence sensors, especially luminous MOFs [31]. MOFs is composed of metal ions or metal clusters and organic ligands. Due to its large specific surface area and controllable pore structure, MOFs has been widely used in catalysis and other fields in recent years [32-34].

Among the most MOFs, Zr-based MOFs possess good chemical properties and thermal stability [35]. UiO-66 series is a typical representative of Zr-based MOFs, which is composed of an inner Zr6O4(OH)4 core and bridged by carboxylates (COO–) deriving from the dicarboxylic ligands to form a Zr6O4(OH)4(CO2)12 cluster [36]. This unique structure makes UiO-66 have broad application prospects in the fields of separation, catalysis, adsorption and so on [37]. Recently, some studies have reported the application of functionalized UiO-66 series of material in fluorescence sensing of phosphoric acid [38,39] and adsorption of phosphoric acid or arsenic acid [40-43]. Inspired by these works, we speculate that UiO-66 series of material could be used for the determination of melamine, because nitrogen, phosphorus, and arsenic belong to the same main group. However, considering that UiO-66 only has a single fluorescence emission peak, the fluorescence changes based on the single peak may be easily affected by the external environment or the sample matrix in the real sample measurement [44]. In recent years, ratiometric fluorescent probes based on MOFs have developed vigorously. This probe generally uses MOFs as the matrix to encapsulate luminescent small molecules such as organic dyes. Then the composite contains the emissions from the host MOFs and the guest dyes, and the two corresponding emission peaks show different responses to the analytes, thus realizing ratiometric luminescence detection [45,46].

Herein, UiO-66-NH2@Ru sensor constructed by encapsulating fluorescent dye of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ into the porous of UiO-66-NH2 through one-pot reaction was described, and its ratiometric sensing property for melamine was investigated initially. As expected, UiO-66-NH2@Ru exhibited dual-fluorescence peak at 445 and 595 nm under the single excitation at 350 nm, corresponding to the emission of UiO-66-NH2 and [Ru(bpy)3]2+, respectively. Based on the overlap between TPA-NH2 emission and [Ru(bpy)3]2+ adsorption spectra, Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) exists between TPA-NH2 and [Ru(bpy)3]2+. This will enhance the fluorescence of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ while quenching the TPA-NH2. Melamine interacts strongly with TPA-NH2 through π-stacking, donor–acceptor interaction, and hydrogen bonding, which kicks out [Ru(bpy)3]2+ and recovered the fluorescence of TPA-NH2, as shown in scheme 1 . Therefore, the phenomenon of luminescence turn-on at 445 nm and turn-off at 595 nm can be clearly observed, and an “on–off” ratiometric fluorescent identification for melamine was established. In addition, UiO-66-NH2@Ru probe also had rapid fluorescent response time, excellent selectivity and sensitivity, wide linear range of 0.27 μM to 110 μM and low detection limit of 90 nM. More importantly, the as-prepared probe was successfully applied to the detection of melamine in spiked infant formula milk with satisfactory results from 90.0% to 98.7%. In conclusion, this study not only provides a new supplementary method for the detection of melamine by MOFs-based nanomaterials, but also proves the feasibility of utilizing Zr-based MOFs as a platform encapsulating organic dye to construct ratiometric fluorescence sensors.

2-Aminoterephthalic acid (TPA-NH2), melamine and tris(2,2-bipyridyl)ruthenium(II) chloride hexahydrate ([Ru(bpy)3]2+) were bought from Aladdin Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Zirconium tetrachloride (ZrCl4) and N,N-dimethylform acid (DMF) were bought from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). UP grade HNO3 was purchased from Crystal Clear Chemical Co., Ltd (Suzhou, China). Ultra‐pure water was purified by the Millipore-Q system (18.2 MΩ cm−1). Wyeth infant milk powder was purchased from the supermarket (Nanchang, China).

The fluorescence spectrum was obtained by the Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectra photometer (Tokyo, Japan) with testing parameters as followed: the excitation wavelength was 350 nm, slit widths were 10 nm, and scan speed was 1200 nm min−1. UV–vis Spectrophotometer was used for UV–vis absorption spectra (U3900, Hitachi). S-3400 N microscope (Hitachi, Japan) was used to acquire scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS). JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan) was applied to obtain transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image. X-ray diffraction (XRD) datas were collected on a D/Max 2500 diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.54056 Å, 40 kV, 200 mA) (Rigaku, Japan). N2 adsorption/desorption isotherm measurements were operated using a BELSORP-mini II instrument (MicrotracBEL, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) in KBr were collected by Irtracer-100 spectrometer (Shimadu, Japan). Inductively coupled plasma-mass spectrometer (ICP-MS) was applied to determine the content of Zr and Ru (Agilent 8800, UK). Multiwave PRO microwave reaction system (Anton parr, Austria).

UiO-66-NH2@Ru was synthesized by one-pot reaction with a little modification according to the previous work of preparing UiO-66-NH2 [36]. 46.6 mg ZrCl4, 36.2 mg TPA-NH2 and 18 mg [Ru(bpy)3]2+ were added to 6 mL DMF, and fully sonicated for 10 min until completely dissolved. Then, the mixture was placed in the oven at 120℃ for 24 h. After the reaction was finished, the suspension was collected by centrifugation (10000 rpm, 6 min), and UiO-66-NH2@Ru product was washed with DMF and methanol for several times and dried in vacuum at 120 ℃ for 10 h. The final product was 76 mg. The preparation process was illustrated in Scheme 1. In addition, the synthesis of UiO-66-NH2 was similar to that of UiO-66-NH2@Ru, except that [Ru(bpy)3]2+ was not added.

12 mg UiO-66-NH2@Ru probe was dispersed in 1 mL ethanol solution, and then 1 μL of the mother solution was added into 999 μL of ultra-pure water with the final concentration of 12 μg mL−1. After incubation at room temperature for a period of time, the standard solution of melamine with different concentration was added to the probe solutions. The emission spectra were recorded upon excitation wavelength at 350 nm after reaction for 3 min. The selectivity of this probe to melamine was confirmed by adding other possible interfering substances instead of melamine in the same way.

In a 5 mL centrifuge tube, 0.2 g of milk powder, 0.5 mL of acetonitrile and 0.5 mL trifluoroacetic acid were added and further ultrasonicated for 5 min. Then followed by the centrifugation at a speed of 10000 rpm for 5 min. The obtained supernatant was neutralized to pH = 7.0 using sodium hydroxide to remove excess trifluoroacetic acid. After filtered through 0.22 μm filter, the final solution was diluted to 5 mL with water for further test [47].

The morphology of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ doped UiO-66-NH2 was determined by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 1 A, the UiO-66-NH2@Ru was irregular with size between 200 and 400 nm, which could be also observed in the high-resolution TEM image of Fig. 1B. Compared with the pure UiO-66-NH2 [48], the edges and corners of UiO-66-NH2@Ru became much smoother, as depicted in Fig. S1 (Supporting Information).

The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was used to characterize the internal molecular crystalline structure and pattern of the synthetic products. As depicted in Fig. 2 A, the peaks of UiO-66-NH2@Ru observed at 7.44°(111), 8.57°(200), 12.13°/14.20°/14.95°(222), 17.12°(400), 25.76°(442) and 30.73°(711), were consistent with the simulated UiO-66 [36,49]. With different [Ru(bpy)3]2+/TPA-NH2 feed ratios, the sharp diffraction peaks of UiO-66-NH2@Ru were similar to UiO-66-NH2, indicating that the effect of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ on the structure of crystallinity could be ignored (Fig.S2 Supporting Information). From the energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) analysis, shown in Fig. 2B, the characteristic elements of C, O, N, Zr appeared in structure, further confirming the successful doping of [Ru(bpy)3]2+.

N2 adsorption and desorption experiment was utilized to characterize the surface area and pore size distribution of the synthetic products. Fig. S3 (Supporting Information) clearly showed that UiO-66-NH2 and UiO-66-NH2@Ru were type I adsorption isotherm without obvious hysteresis. The ascent curve at high pressure was likely to be caused by inaccurate free space. The BET surface area of UiO-66-NH2 was 653 m2 g−1, which approximated to the reported data, such as 617 m2 g−1 [50] and 698 m2 g−1 [43]. But the BET value of UiO-66-NH2@Ru decreased to 581 m2 g−1, indicating that [Ru(bpy)3]2+ may be involved in coordination in one-pot synthesis or may be directly encapsulated in the pores of UiO-66-NH2@Ru rather than on the surface [23]. In addition, when the spherical [Ru(bpy)3]2+ molecule (~12 Å in size) entered into the pore, the pore size of UiO-66-NH2@Ru slightly reduced from 1.7 nm to 1.6 nm. From the FT-IR spectra of UiO-66-NH2@Ru composite (Fig. 2C), both characteristic vibration peaks, Zr-O group vibration (663 cm−1), Zr-O–H group vibration (1386 cm−1) and C-O-Zr group vibration (1437 and 1571 cm−1), belonged to UiO-66-NH2 were found [51]. The peaks at 3451 cm−1 and 3367 cm−1 indicated the existence of the amine moieties from the ligand [40], but no obvious peak of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ was observed in UiO-66-NH2@Ru. The above results further proved that [Ru(bpy)3]2+ was incorporated in UiO-66-NH2 without damaging its structure, and UiO-66-NH2@Ru was prepared successfully.

The optical properties of UiO-66-NH2@Ru, TPA-NH2 ligand and [Ru(bpy)3]2+ were studied. As depicted in Fig. 3 A, UiO-66-NH2@Ru has three absorption peaks at 215, 276 and 388 nm, which basically corresponded to the absorption of TPA-NH2 [52] and [Ru(bpy)3]2+. Meanwhile, UiO-66-NH2@Ru exhibits significant fluorescence emission intensity dependent on excitation wavelength properties, as described in Fig. 3B. With increase of exciting wavelength, on one hand, the emission peak intensity of UiO-66-NH2 ascended gradually, but attenuated when the excitation wavelength was greater than 350 nm. On the other hand, the intensity of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ decreased firstly and then increased gradually, and inclined to be stable at 350 nm finally. So the optimal excitation wavelength was set at 350 nm. As illustrated in Fig. 3C, UiO-66-NH2@Ru also has two emission peaks at 445 nm and 595 nm under 350 nm excitation. In comparison with the ligand, UiO-66-NH2 has a red shift to 445 nm, while [Ru(bpy)3]2+ had a blue shift to 595 nm, indicating a possible energy transfer between them (Fig. S4, Supporting Information) [23]. Therefore, UiO-66-NH2@Ru composite combines the optical properties of both [Ru(bpy)3]2+ and UiO-66-NH2. The stability of UiO-66-NH2@Ru in solution was also investigated. As shown in Fig. 3D, it has good day-to-day stability for at least two weeks, which provides a prerequisite for the detection of melamine in aqueous solution as a probe.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility of determining melamine based on UiO-66-NH2@Ru
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of UiO-66-NH2@Ru on melamine was investigated in aqueous solutions. It was found that the emission intensity of UiO-66-NH2 increased greatly while that of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ decreased slightly (Fig. S5, Supporting Information), which made the ratio of F445/F595 increasing obviously. Therefore, this dual-emission probe provided a feasibility for the ratiometric fluorescent determine of melamine. In order to improve the detection sensitivity, the effects of the proportion of materials, the probe concentration and the reaction time were all discussed as below. Firstly, the molar ratio of TPA-NH2/Zr was fixed at 1:1 and the ratio of [Ru(bpy)3]2+/TPA-NH2 was investigated to prepare a series of complexes with different Ru loading, and their luminescence properties were further studied. When the ratio of Ru/TPA-NH2 increased from 0.06 to 0.21, the actual loading of Ru was obtained from 1.90 wt% to 3.52 wt% by ICP-MS after microwave digestion by HNO3 for 20 min (Table S1). It can be seen from Fig. S6 (Supporting Information) that the fluorescence intensity of UiO-66-NH2 was negatively correlated to the Ru loading, which was attributed to the FRET between TPA-NH2 and [Ru(bpy)3]2+ (Fig. S7, Supporting Information). However, the luminescence intensity of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ increased with the increasing of the ratio of Ru/TPA-NH2, but decreased slightly when the ratio is bigger than 0.12:1, which was due to the fact that the loading of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ reached its maximum. Therefore 0.12:1 was selected as the feed ratio for preparing UiO-66-NH2@Ru, and the corresponding loading of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ in UiO-66-NH2@Ru was 3.52 wt%. Secondly, the concentration of UiO-66-NH2@Ru probe was optimized. As shown in Fig. 4 A, with the increase of UiO-66-NH2@Ru concentration from 2 to 20 μg mL−1, the fluorescence intensity at 445 nm and 595 nm was gradually enhanced, with no significant increase at about 12 μg mL−1. And the F445/F595 value also appeared an inflection point at this concentration, seen in Fig. S8 (Supporting Information). Thus 12 μg mL−1 was chosen as the preferred concentration for probe use. Finally, the reaction time was also studied to determine whether the reaction was complete. Fig. 4B showed that the reaction time between UiO-66-NH2@Ru and melamine was fast and reached a balance within 3 min. Thus 3 min was selected as the optimum time of the probe to ensure that the analyst could fully enter the MOFs channel and reacted with it totally.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent determination of melamine based on UiO-66-NH2@Ru
ce:section.ce:para: The ratiometric fluorescent measurement of melamine based on UiO-66-NH2@Ru was investigated according to the above optimized conditions. The results were shown in Fig. 5 . With the increase of melamine concentration, the luminescence of UiO-66-NH2 at 445 nm gradually increased, while the intensity of 595 nm belonged to [Ru(bpy)3]2+ slowly decreased. The corresponding fluorescence ratio (F445/F595) increased linearly with the increasing concentration of melamine in the range of 0.27–110 μM, and the linear equation was obtained by fitting the working curve. Based on the singal to noise ratio of 3, LOD of melamine was evaluated to be 90 nM (equivalent to 0.011 mg/kg), which was lower than the maximum levels in powdered infant formula (1.0 mg/kg) and other foods or animal feed (2.5 mg/kg). In comparison with other reported nanomaterials listed in Table 1 , the ratiometric fluorescence sensor in this work had a lower detection limit or a wider linear range with the simpler preparation method for UiO-66-NH2@Ru [22,25,53-56].

Selectivity is an important feature of probe in practical application, so the selective investigations on the possible interfering substances were conducted. The results were shown in Fig. S9 (Supporting Information). Only after the addition of melamine, the F445/F595 value was increased significantly, while other substances including Na+, K+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cu2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, NH4 +, SO4 2−, CO3 2−, Cl−, Br−, glucose, l-glutamic, lysine, l-cysteine and tryptophan, would not cause any changes. This indicated that UiO-66-NH2@Ru has high selectivity toward melamine and good application potential in real sample.

The luminescent response caused by melamine can be analyzed in the following four aspects. First, powder XRD of the recovered UiO-66-NH2@Ru after sensing melamine remained intact, indicating that the skeleton of UiO-66-NH2 had not collapsed (Fig. S10, Supporting Information). Secondly, the UV–vis absorption spectra of melamine and the excitation spectra of the UiO-66-NH2@Ru complex were compared. It was found that the overlap between them was negligible, indicating that there was no energy transfer between them (Fig. S11, Supporting Information). Third, based on the overlap between TPA-NH2 emission and [Ru(bpy)3]2+ adsorption spectra, FRET exists between TPA-NH2 and [Ru(bpy)3]2+ (Fig.S7, Supporting Information). When melamine was adsorbed on framework ligand, the melamine can strongly interact with TPA-NH2 by π-stacking, donor–acceptor interaction and hydrogen bonding, which disrupted the energy transfer of TPA-NH2 to [Ru(bpy)3]2+, resulting in enhanced emission of TPA-NH2 and decreased emission of [Ru(bpy)3]2+ [57,58]. Finally, it can be seen from Fig. S12 (Supporting Information) that the fluorescence of UiO-66-NH2 also shows an increasing trend after adding melamine, which further indicates that the UiO-66-NH2 MOFs plays an important role in the “on–off” ratiometric sensor. Eventually, an obvious ratiometric fluorescent method was developed for the determination of melamine.

Sample pretreatments were described in Section 2.5. In order to testify the accuracy of this method, the standard addition method was utilized to measure melamine in milk powder. The results showed that good precision and high recovery was obtained within the range of 93.0%–98.7%. Under the same conditions, the relative standard deviations of three repeated measurements were all below 4.26%, indicating good precision of this method (Table S2). Therefore, the UiO-66-NH2@Ru probe would have great potential applicability in milk powder sample.

Through one-pot reaction, the porous Zr-based MOFs was used to encapsulate [Ru(bpy)3]2+ molecule to form the composite probe of UiO-66-NH2@Ru with dual emission signals, which could constitute the ratiometric fluorescent sensor to detect melamine sensitively and selectively. Upon the addition of melamine, thefluorescence intensity at 445 nm enhanced along with decreased intensity at 595 nm. To our knowledge, this is the first example of MOFs as a fluorescent “on–off” probe toward melamine, which showed a wide linear range, low detection limit and quick response. What’s more, the experiment proved that UiO-66-NH2@Ru probe can be developed to detect melamine in actual sample of milk powder with satisfying results. Therefore, this study will be important and beneficial for the design or development of MOFs used as “on-off” ratiometric fluorescence sensors in melamine detection.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21465015 and 21964010).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2020.105837.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Shellaiah, K.W Sun, Review on nanomaterial-based melamine detection, Chemosensors, 7 (2019) 9-55.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enzymatic fuel cell-based self-powered homogeneous immunosensing platform via target-induced glucose release: an appealing alternative strategy for turn-on melamine assay

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.C. Gu, P.P. Gai, T. Hou, H.Y. Li, C.H. Xue, F. Li, Enzymatic fuel cell-based self-powered homogeneous immunosensing platform via target-induced glucose release: an appealing alternative strategy for turn-on melamine assay, ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 9 (2017) 35721-35728.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The natural outcome of melamine-induced bladder stones with bladder epithelial hyperplasia after the withdrawal of melamine in mice
